FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study baseline data (2016-2018), N=10,650 children aged 9.9±0.6 years, recruited via probability sampling from USA population

FT_IC2.evidence: Paper measures specific screen media variables separately: TV watching, video watching (e.g., YouTube), playing online games, texting, social media (Twitter, Instagram), video chat (Facetime), and mature-rated games/movies. Assessed via Youth Screen Time Survey with 7-point Likert scale (none, <30 min, 30 min, 1h, 2h, 3h, ≥4h) for both weekday and weekend days. Per FT-IC2 qualifying criteria: platform-specific social media (Twitter, Instagram) and age of acquisition of phone/app use measured.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multilevel general linear models and multilevel logistic regression testing associations between parental psychological problems (externalizing, internalizing, total) and children's screen time outcomes. Per Methods: 'Multilevel general linear models to examine if presence of parental psychological problems was associated with children's daily screen time, measured as minutes per day. Multilevel logistic regression models were used to examine if presence of parental psychological problems was associated with the binary outcome of children meeting versus not meeting the recommendation of two daily hours or less.' Results section reports regression coefficients and ORs across multiple screen media types (TV, videos, games, social media).

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric values reported for TV, videos, games, and mature-rated media outcomes. Examples from Table 4: 'Parental internalising problems were associated with children watching mature-rated movies (OR = 1.14, 95% CI = 1.00, 1.30) and playing mature-rated games (OR = 1.27, 95% CI = 1.11, 1.45).' Table 2 reports coefficients with 95% CIs for total screen time minutes per day.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis of ABCD baseline data (2016-2018) with inferential statistics (multilevel regression, logistic regression). Per Methods: 'We used multilevel general linear models to examine if presence of parental psychological problems was associated with children's daily screen time' and 'We used logistic regression analyses to examine if presence of parental psychological problem was associated with children's use of mature-rated games (no/yes) or movies (no/yes).'

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed journal article in Acta Paediatrica (2022), DOI 10.1111/apa.16253; no evidence of duplicate/preprint status

FT_EC7_reason: FT-EC7 does NOT apply. Paper is CROSS-SECTIONAL (not longitudinal/prospective), failing criterion (c). Additionally, parental psychological problems include clinical dimensions (externalizing, internalizing pathology) which are individual psychological constructs, not pure demographics (SES, race, sex, age, parental education). Per criterion (b), FT-EC7 applies ONLY when predictors are ONLY demographic/contextual. This paper includes psychological/clinical predictors → keep INCLUDE.
notes: Key difference from C1: Paper DOES measure specific SM/screen variables separately (Tables 3-4), not just aggregate composite. C2 correct on FT-IC2 MET. Directionality (parental psych → screen time, not SM → outcome) does NOT trigger FT-EC7 exclusion due to cross-sectional design and inclusion of individual psychological predictors.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data, first three waves, N=5,596 youth aged 9-10 years (47.2% female, 55.5% non-Hispanic White). Abstract: 'Data were from the first three waves of the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study.'

FT_IC2.evidence: Paper measures cyber victimization but provides no evidence of a separately-measured SPECIFIC smartphone or social media variable. Abstract states 'experiences of peer (overt, relational, reputational) and cyber victimization' but does not cite measurement of SM time, app use, platform-specific SM use, SM account ownership, or SM-specific metrics. Per edge case E6: 'Cyberbullying — paper also measures phone/SM separately → MET. Cyberbullying only → NOT_MET.' Cyber victimization is measured in isolation with no independently reported phone/SM exposure variable. No PDF available to verify; abstract provides no evidence of distinct phone/SM measurement.

FT_IC3.evidence: Mixed-effects logistic regression with reported coefficients and ORs: 'SGM youth experienced elevated peer (overt: β = 0.40, 95% CI 0.31-0.49; relational: β = 0.43, 95% CI 0.34-0.53; reputational: β = 0.51, 95% CI 0.42-0.60) and cyber victimization (OR 2.35, 95% CI 1.77-3.11).' Mediation analysis: 'Around 22%-28% of the disparities in current suicidal ideation or attempts were mediated by peer or cyber victimization.' Inferential tests linking cyber victimization (as predictor/mediator) to suicidal ideation/attempts (non-SM outcomes).

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple extractable numerics reported: OR = 2.35 (95% CI 1.77-3.11) for cyber victimization; β coefficients for peer victimization types (0.40, 0.43, 0.51); mediation percentages (22%-28%); ORs for suicidal outcomes (4.75, 5.87, 4.94 with CIs).

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis: mixed-effects logistic regression examining waves 1-2 predictors (SGM identity) predicting wave 3 outcomes (suicidality), with longitudinal mediation analysis testing whether peer and cyber victimization mediate this association. Per abstract: 'mixed-effects logistic regression to quantify the association between SGM identity reported at waves 1-2 (9-11 years) and suicidal ideation and attempts at wave 3 (11-12 years) and longitudinal mediation analysis.'

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed journal article in Journal of Child Psychology and Psychiatry (2025), DOI 10.1111/jcpp.14155; no evidence of duplicate or preprint status

FT_EC7_reason: FT-EC7 does NOT apply. Paper fails FT-IC2 (no separately-measured specific phone/SM variable). Additionally, even if cyber victimization qualified as a phone/SM variable, FT-EC7 would not apply because: (a) paper includes individual psychological predictor (SGM identity — sexual and gender minority status is an individual characteristic, not demographic-only), and (b) paper reports mediation pathways and downstream outcomes (suicidality), not merely demographic-only prospective prediction of SM use.
notes: C1 correctly identified FT-IC2 NOT_MET per E6 rule. C2 incorrectly assumed cyber victimization alone qualifies as phone/SM variable without independent SM measurement. Cyber victimization ≠ social media use (E6 clear: paper must measure phone/SM separately). Paper fails on FT-IC2, which is a tier-1 inclusion criterion.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study data, N=1,985 adolescents, Year 2-Year 4 follow-up (mean age Year 2 = 11.91 years). Abstract: 'We examined prospective associations between self-reported sleep duration, social media use and brain activation in 1985 adolescents (mean age, Year 2 = 11.91 years) from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study.'

FT_IC2.evidence: Social media use measured via Youth Screen Time Survey (self-reported). Abstract: 'social media use via the Youth Screen Time Survey' — represents specific platform-independent SM variable qualifying under FT-IC2 criteria ('Social media: time, platform-specific or aggregate account-based measures').

FT_IC3.evidence: Prospective regression models testing bidirectional associations between social media and sleep with moderation by brain activation: 'We tested three sets of prospective models to examine how sleep, social media use, executive control and reward-related brain activation influence each other over two years.' Models include: SM → sleep (Year 2 SM predicting Year 4 sleep), sleep → SM (Year 2 sleep predicting Year 4 SM), moderated by fMRI activation in multiple brain regions. Per FT-IC3: 'Paper reports an inferential statistical test linking the smartphone/SM variable to at least one non-SM variable (in either direction)' — sleep is a non-SM outcome; associations reported both directions.

FT_IC4.evidence: No PDF available. Abstract describes associations qualitatively ('Greater social media use at Year 2 predicted shorter sleep duration at Year 4, particularly among adolescents with lower activation in the nucleus accumbens, cingulate gyrus, insula and putamen. Shorter sleep also predicted increased social media use two years later, with effects moderated by activation in the insula and middle frontal gyrus.') but does NOT report numeric values (β, OR, p-values, t-statistics, F-statistics) for the SM-sleep associations. Moderation effects described qualitatively only. Per FT-IC4: 'At least one β / OR / HR / r / CI / F / t / χ² / p-value reported' — abstract does not provide these for SM associations; cannot verify supplementary materials without PDF.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal ABCD analysis with prospective models across two years (Year 2 to Year 4) testing associations between social media use, sleep duration, and brain activation (fMRI during Monetary Incentive Delay task). Per abstract: 'prospective models to examine how sleep, social media use, executive control and reward-related brain activation influence each other over two years.'

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed journal article in Journal of Sleep Research (2026), DOI 10.1111/jsr.70325; no evidence of duplicate or preprint

FT_EC7_reason: FT-EC7 does NOT apply for multiple reasons: (1) Paper reports bidirectional associations between SM and sleep (SM as both IV and DV across different models), violating criterion (a) which requires SM as DV 'in every reported analysis.' (2) Paper includes individual psychological/neural predictors (brain activation in reward regions, executive control regions: nucleus accumbens, cingulate gyrus, insula, putamen, caudate, middle frontal gyrus) — criterion (b) requires ONLY demographic/contextual predictors. Per criterion (b): 'The predictors are ONLY demographic or contextual (SES, race, sex, age, income, parental education, gender identity, sexual orientation, urbanicity, neighborhood, parenting, family structure)' — neural features disqualify FT-EC7.
notes: C1 and C2 disagree on FT-IC4: C1 MET (cites qualitative description as evidence), C2 UNKNOWN (correctly notes no numeric coefficients in abstract without PDF). Per E12: 'Figure-only — numeric supplement exists → MET; no numerics → UNKNOWN.' Without PDF and no reported coefficients in abstract, FT-IC4 is UNKNOWN. This is a close call given abstract's qualitative descriptions, but strict reading of FT-IC4 requires explicit numeric values. Paper may still be INCLUDABLE if it passes other criteria and numeric data are in full text/tables.
